does: This paper introduces RepairBench, a leaderboard and evaluation framework for measuring the performance of frontier AI models on automated program repair tasks. The leaderboard employs execution-based evaluation, meaning all generated patches are compiled and tested against real test suites rather than using superficial metrics. RepairBench evaluates large language models and specialized repair systems on two established, high-quality benchmarks: Defects4J, which contains real bugs from Java projects, and GitBug-Java, which provides additional real-world repair scenarios. The authors establish a standardized, reproducible evaluation protocol and commit to regularly updating the leaderboard as new frontier models emerge, enabling ongoing comparison of state-of-the-art progress in AI-driven program repair.
connects: This work builds on decades of automated program repair research that has progressed from template-based and search-based techniques to neural approaches. It extends prior benchmarking efforts like Defects4J and GitBug-Java, which were developed to provide realistic bug datasets for repair evaluation. The paper responds to the rapid advancement of large language models and their application to code understanding and generation tasks, recognizing that standardized leaderboards have proven effective in driving progress in other AI domains like machine translation and question answering. RepairBench addresses a gap in the program repair community where evaluation has been fragmented across different papers using different subsets of benchmarks, making it difficult to track genuine progress as models evolve.
enables: By providing a frequent, standardized leaderboard with real execution-based feedback, RepairBench creates infrastructure for systematic benchmarking that can guide the development of repair models and identify which architectural choices and training approaches work best. It enables researchers to quickly assess whether improvements in general-purpose language models translate to better repair performance, and to detect when domain-specific approaches are still necessary. The execution-based evaluation framework provides rich feedback that could be used to develop better repair models that understand test-driven development. The public release of the framework and regular updates could catalyze community effort toward solving remaining program repair challenges, similar to how leaderboards have accelerated progress in other areas of AI.
